Francis Godwin, Director, Office of Manufacturing Quality, Office of Compliance, Center for Drug Evaluation and Research.
ATTN: Zachary Bogorad; reply email CDER-OC-OMQ-Communications@fda.hhs.gov.
September 16 to 20, 2024.
October 11, 2024.
August 7, 2023.

In source order: (1) Finished product original release testing data; (2) Original stability testing data; (3) Sample collection and preparation; (4) Procedures for quality control data review (e.g., raw material, finished product release, and stability testing); (5) Batch production and control record.
Import Alert 66-40.
Section 501(a)(2)(B) of the FD&C Act, 21 U.S.C. 351(a)(2)(B).
Inadequate. FDA states the response does not adequately evaluate the full scope of data integrity (DI) deficiencies throughout the firm's drug product manufacturing operations.
The firm's impact assessment commitment was limited to missing (b)(4) test data and stability.

The response failed to address the potential impacts on all drug product lots distributed to the United States that remain within expiry and were manufactured without documentation of testing performed.
The Laboratory Manager stated that the firm fabricated several laboratory investigations that were not performed.
15 working days.
143 days. From March 17, 2023 to August 7, 2023: 14 days remaining in March + 30 (April) + 31 (May) + 30 (June) + 31 (July) + 7 (August 1-7) = 143 days.
21 days. From September 20, 2024 to October 11, 2024: 10 days remaining in September + 11 days in October = 21 days.
Extensive annual audits for at least 2 years, conducted by a qualified consultant, after the firm has executed its DI remediation protocol.

171 days. From September 20, 2024 to March 10, 2025: 10 days remaining in September + 31 (October) + 30 (November) + 31 (December) + 31 (January 2025) + 28 (February 2025) + 10 (March 1-10) = 171 days.

21 CFR 211.68(b) (violation 2). The letter says the firm's laboratory equipment used to generate analytical data for finished drug product release and stability testing lacked restricted access and sufficient controls.
